summary: American landscape architect (1822–1903), co-designer of Central Park and Prospect Park with Calvert Vaux. Known for the Greensward Plan, the Emerald Necklace in Boston, the Midway Plaisance for the 1893 World's Fair, and the Biltmore Estate grounds. Mentor Andrew Jackson Downing introduced him to Vaux. Also authored The Cotton Kingdom about antebellum Southern poverty.

works.name: Central Park
works.description: Designed with Calvert Vaux, who became his partner after Olmsted's mentor Andrew Jackson Downing introduced them. Their design, the Greensward Plan, beat out engineer Ebert Ludovicus Viele's existing plan. Landmarks include the Bethesda Fountain, the Sheep Meadow, the Bow Bridge, the Great Lawn, and a zoo. The park is the setting of Edward Albee's play The Zoo Story. Robert Smithson's 'Dialectical Landscape' article centered on Olmsted's Greensward Plan. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Co-designed with Calvert Vaux using the Greensward Plan; contains the Bethesda Fountain, the Sheep Meadow, the Bow Bridge, and the Great Lawn
examples: With that partner, this architect used the Greensward Plan to create a location with such landmarks as Bethesda Fountain and the Sheep Meadow.
Olmsted and Vaux designed this location in Manhattan. It receives numerous tourists, contains a zoo, and runs open-air theater and concert productions each summer.

clues.clue: Setting of Edward Albee's play The Zoo Story
examples: Olmsted worked with Calvert Vaux on this New York City landmark, which is the setting of Edward Albee's play The Zoo Story.

clues.clue: Robert Smithson's 'Dialectical Landscape' article centers on Olmsted and reveres the Greensward Plan
examples: Smithson's article about the 'Dialectical Landscape' centers on this man, whose Greensward Plan Smithson revered.

clues.clue: Ebert Ludovicus Viele was the original engineer-in-chief for the project
examples: This American's best-known project featured Ebert Ludovicus Viele as the engineer-in-chief.

works.name: Emerald Necklace (Boston)
works.description: A chain of parks and parkways in Boston, Massachusetts, often called the Emerald Necklace, designed by Olmsted to connect several green spaces through linked pathways. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Chain of parks in Boston connecting several green spaces; called the Emerald Necklace
examples: name the landscape architect who designed the Emerald Necklace in Boston
The very extensive designs of this man include Boston's Emerald Necklace

works.name: 1893 World's Columbian Exposition / Midway Plaisance
works.description: Olmsted designed the grounds for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, including the Midway Plaisance (an entertainment corridor) and a Wooded Island at the center. He originally wanted a canal running through the Midway Plaisance. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed the grounds (including the Midway Plaisance and the Wooded Island) for the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago
examples: This longtime partner of Calvert Vaux designed the grounds of the 1893 World's Fair in Chicago
Frederick Law Olmsted designed a Wooded Island as a centerpiece of this event.
This man wanted to create a canal running through the Midway Plaisance he designed for the exposition.

works.name: Biltmore Estate Grounds
works.description: Olmsted designed the landscape for the Biltmore Estate in Asheville, North Carolina (built by Richard Morris Hunt for George Vanderbilt). His care for the surrounding forests inadvertently established the groundwork for American forestry; Carl A. Schenck taught at the nation's first forest school there. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Designed grounds for the Biltmore Estate (buildings by Richard Morris Hunt); his care for surrounding forests set the groundwork for forestry in the U.S.
examples: His care for the forests surrounding the grounds of the Biltmore Estate that he designed inadvertently set the groundwork for forestry in the U.S.
Olmsted was the landscape architect for this estate, whose buildings were designed by Richard Morris Hunt.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Prospect Park in Brooklyn (with Calvert Vaux); campus of Stanford University; campus of the University of Chicago; planned city of Riverside, Illinois; Buffalo park system and grounds of the Buffalo State Hospital (with H.H. Richardson); U.S. Capitol Grounds.

clues.clue: Prospect Park in Brooklyn (co-designed with Calvert Vaux)
examples: He partnered with Calvert Vaux to create Prospect Park.

Both the grounds of Richardson's asylum and an extensive connected park system in Buffalo were designed by this landscape architect, who worked with his partner Calvert Vaux on New York's Prospect and Central Parks.

clues.clue: Designed the campus of Stanford University; also the U.S. Capitol Grounds and the University of Chicago campus
examples: This longtime partner of Calvert Vaux designed the grounds of the 1893 World's Fair in Chicago and the campus of Stanford University.

clues.clue: Designed the grounds of the Buffalo State Hospital with H.H. Richardson; also designed the Buffalo park system
examples: Both the grounds of Richardson's asylum and an extensive connected park system in Buffalo were designed by this landscape architect

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Frederick Law Olmsted (1822–1903) was a landscape architect, author, conservationist, and public servant. Before his design career he was a merchant, apprentice seaman, experimental farmer, and goldmine manager. He was co-founder of The Nation magazine. His mentor Andrew Jackson Downing introduced him to partner Calvert Vaux. He served as chairman of the first Yosemite Commission. He authored The Cotton Kingdom (a.k.a. Journeys and Explorations in the Cotton Kingdom), an account of antebellum Southern poverty arguing that slavery made the South economically inefficient. Near the end of his life he was committed to McLean Hospital in Waverly, Massachusetts—whose grounds he had himself designed.

clues.clue: Mentor Andrew Jackson Downing introduced him to Calvert Vaux
examples: This man, who had no experience in design at the time, met his English-born partner thanks to his mentor Andrew Jackson Downing.
Andrew Jackson Downing introduced this man to his collaborator Calvert Vaux.

clues.clue: Authored The Cotton Kingdom / Journeys and Explorations in the Cotton Kingdom, arguing slavery made the South economically inefficient
examples: This man argued that slavery made the South economically inefficient in his account of touring the antebellum South, Journeys and Explorations in the Cotton Kingdom.
He described antebellum Southern poverty in his book Journeys and Explorations in the Cotton Kingdom.

clues.clue: Near end of life, was ironically committed to McLean Hospital in Waverly, Massachusetts — whose grounds he had designed
examples: Near the end of his life, this man was ironically committed to McLean Hospital in Waverly, Massachusetts, whose grounds he had designed.
This cofounder of The Nation collaborated on the Buffalo State Hospital with his friend Henry Hobson Richardson

clues.clue: Chairman of the first Yosemite Commission
examples: Identify this chairman of the first Yosemite Commission, who was the landscape architect for the World's Columbia Exposition and Stanford University.

clues.clue: Before design career: was a merchant, apprentice seaman, experimental farmer, and goldmine manager

comprehensive_summary:
Frederick Law Olmsted (1822–1903) was the founding figure of American landscape architecture, best known for co-designing Central Park in Manhattan with his English-born partner Calvert Vaux. Their winning submission, the Greensward Plan, beat out a design by engineer Ebert Ludovicus Viele. The park's landmarks include the Bethesda Fountain, the Sheep Meadow, the Bow Bridge, the Great Lawn, and a zoo. It is the setting of Edward Albee's play The Zoo Story, and Robert Smithson's essay 'Dialectical Landscape' centers on Olmsted's Greensward Plan. Olmsted and Vaux also co-designed Prospect Park in Brooklyn.

Olmsted's Boston work produced the Emerald Necklace, a chain of parks and parkways linking several green spaces across the city. For the 1893 World's Columbian Exposition in Chicago, he designed the grounds including the Midway Plaisance (an entertainment corridor) and a Wooded Island at its center, though he originally wanted to cut a canal through the Midway. He also designed the campus of Stanford University, the University of Chicago campus, the U.S. Capitol Grounds, the planned city of Riverside, Illinois, and the Buffalo park system and the grounds of the Buffalo State Hospital alongside his friend H.H. Richardson.

Olmsted designed the landscape for the Biltmore Estate in North Carolina, the buildings of which were by Richard Morris Hunt. His care for the estate's surrounding forests inadvertently helped lay the groundwork for American forestry, and the nation's first forest school was located there.

Before his design career, Olmsted had been a merchant, apprentice seaman, experimental farmer, and goldmine manager. He was co-founder of The Nation magazine and chairman of the first Yosemite Commission. His mentor, Andrew Jackson Downing (who died in a steamboat explosion on the Hudson River), introduced him to Calvert Vaux. Olmsted also wrote The Cotton Kingdom (formally Journeys and Explorations in the Cotton Kingdom), arguing that slavery made the antebellum South economically inefficient. Near the end of his life he was committed to McLean Hospital in Waverly, Massachusetts—whose grounds he had himself designed.
